∑ ArithmetiQ

Mathematics made simple — every step, every time.

Free, fully worked solutions for NCTB and Edexcel students. No skipped steps. No assumed knowledge. Just clear, friendly maths.

Choose Your Course

Every solution written from scratch — as if you've never seen the topic before.

📐 SSC · Class 9–10 NCTB General Math 9–10 Complete solutions for the NCTB Class 9–10 General Mathematics textbook. Every exercise, every question — worked step by step, perfect for SSC preparation. 🟢 Beginner friendly Start Learning → 📊 SSC · Class 9–10 NCTB Higher Math 9–10 Algebra, trigonometry, geometry and calculus from the NCTB Higher Math syllabus — all broken down from first principles with zero assumptions. 🟡 Intermediate Start Learning → 📘 A-Level · Pure 1 Edexcel Pure Mathematics 1 Functions, coordinate geometry, binomial expansion, calculus and more. Edexcel P1 fully solved — every worked example explained at the level of a complete beginner. 🟡 Intermediate Start Learning → 📗 A-Level · Pure 2 Edexcel Pure Mathematics 2 Building on P1 — sequences, trigonometric identities, integration by parts, differential equations. Every technique explained from the ground up. 🔴 Advanced Start Learning → How It Works 🎯 Pick a topic Navigate to any chapter in your course and choose a question or exercise. → 📖 Read the solution Every step is numbered, named, and explained — not just algebra on a page. → 💡 Understand, not memorise Walk away knowing the why behind every answer, not just the answer itself. "If you can read, you can follow along — no matter your level."

ArithmetiQ will never skip a step, never say "it's obvious that…", and never leave you stranded. Every student deserves clear, patient, thorough mathematics help.

Completely free. Always.

docs.title: Exercise 1.1 — Real Numbers
docs.text:
Question 1 of 13 Next ▶ Question 1 🟢 Easy

Which of the following numbers is irrational?

  1. \( 0.\dot{3} \)
  2. \( \sqrt{\dfrac{16}{9}} \)
  3. \( \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{8}{27}} \)
  4. \( \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{3}} \)
Theory — Rational and Irrational Numbers A rational number can be written as \( \dfrac{p}{q} \), where \( p \) and \( q \) are integers and \( q \neq 0 \). This includes all integers, all terminating decimals, and all recurring decimals (since they repeat a fixed pattern). An irrational number cannot be written as \( \dfrac{p}{q} \). Its decimal expansion goes on forever with no repeating pattern. Examples: \( \sqrt{2},\ \sqrt{3},\ \pi \). Key tests: • If \( \sqrt{n} \) simplifies to a whole number or a fraction → rational. • If \( \sqrt{n} \) cannot simplify (because \( n \) is not a perfect square) → irrational. • A non-zero rational ÷ an irrational = irrational. Strategy Simplify each option completely. If it reduces to a fraction or integer, it is rational. If an irreducible square (or cube) root remains, it is irrational. Step 1 — Simplify option 1: \( 0.\dot{3} \) \( 0.\dot{3} \) means \( 0.3333\ldots \) — a recurring decimal. Every recurring decimal equals a fraction: \[ 0.\dot{3} = \frac{1}{3} \] This is a ratio of two integers. It is rational. Step 2 — Simplify option 2: \( \sqrt{\dfrac{16}{9}} \) Take the square root of the numerator and denominator separately: \[ \sqrt{\frac{16}{9}} = \frac{\sqrt{16}}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{4}{3} \] Both 16 and 9 are perfect squares, so the result is a simple fraction. It is rational. Step 3 — Simplify option 3: \( \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{8}{27}} \) Take the cube root of numerator and denominator separately: \[ \sqrt[3]{\frac{8}{27}} = \frac{\sqrt[3]{8}}{\sqrt[3]{27}} = \frac{2}{3} \] Since \( 2^3 = 8 \) and \( 3^3 = 27 \), both are perfect cubes. Result is a fraction. It is rational. Step 4 — Simplify option 4: \( \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{3}} \) The denominator contains \( \sqrt{3} \). Since 3 is not a perfect square, \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational — it cannot be expressed as a fraction. Dividing the rational number 5 by the irrational \( \sqrt{3} \) always gives an irrational result. Therefore \( \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{3}} \) is irrational. Final Answer The answer is option 4. The number \( \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{3}} \) is irrational. Options 1–3 are all rational: \( 0.\dot{3} = \dfrac{1}{3} \), \( \sqrt{\dfrac{16}{9}} = \dfrac{4}{3} \), \( \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{8}{27}} = \dfrac{2}{3} \). Question 1 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 2 of 13 Next ▶ Question 2 🟢 Easy

State whether each of the following is rational or irrational, giving a reason.

  1. \( 0.\dot{4} \)
  2. \( \sqrt{9} \)
  3. \( \sqrt{11} \)
  4. \( \dfrac{\sqrt{27}}{\sqrt{48}} \)
  5. \( 5.\dot{6}3\dot{9} \)
Strategy For each number, ask: can it be written as \( \dfrac{p}{q} \)? • Recurring decimals → always rational (they convert to fractions). • \( \sqrt{n} \) → rational if \( n \) is a perfect square, irrational otherwise. • A ratio of surds: simplify first — the surds may cancel. Step 1 — Classify \( 0.\dot{4} \) \( 0.\dot{4} = 0.4444\ldots \) is a recurring decimal. All recurring decimals are rational: \[ 0.\dot{4} = \frac{4}{9} \] It is rational. Step 2 — Classify \( \sqrt{9} \) Since \( 3^2 = 9 \), the number 9 is a perfect square: \[ \sqrt{9} = 3 \] The result is a whole number, which is rational. It is rational. Step 3 — Classify \( \sqrt{11} \) The perfect squares either side of 11 are \( 3^2 = 9 \) and \( 4^2 = 16 \). Since 11 lies strictly between them, it is not a perfect square. Therefore \( \sqrt{11} \) cannot be simplified to a fraction. It is irrational. Step 4 — Classify \( \dfrac{\sqrt{27}}{\sqrt{48}} \) Combine the square roots into one: \[ \frac{\sqrt{27}}{\sqrt{48}} = \sqrt{\frac{27}{48}} \] Simplify the fraction (GCD of 27 and 48 is 3): \[ \sqrt{\frac{27}{48}} = \sqrt{\frac{9}{16}} = \frac{\sqrt{9}}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{3}{4} \] The irrational parts cancelled. The result is a simple fraction. It is rational. Step 5 — Classify \( 5.\dot{6}3\dot{9} \) The notation \( 5.\dot{6}3\dot{9} \) means the digits 6, 3, 9 repeat: \( 5.639639639\ldots \) This is a recurring decimal, so it can be written as a fraction. It is rational. Final Answer Rational: \( 0.\dot{4} \), \( \sqrt{9} \), \( \dfrac{\sqrt{27}}{\sqrt{48}} \), \( 5.\dot{6}3\dot{9} \) Irrational: \( \sqrt{11} \) ◀ Previous Question 2 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 3 of 13 Next ▶ Question 3 🟢 Easy

Convert the following into recurring decimal fractions:

  1. \( \dfrac{1}{6} \)
  2. \( \dfrac{7}{11} \)
  3. \( 3\dfrac{8}{15} \)
Theory — Recurring Decimals When you divide one integer by another using long division, the remainder eventually repeats — because there are only finitely many possible remainders. Once a remainder repeats, the decimal digits from that point on repeat forever. This is called a recurring (repeating) decimal. Notation: a dot above a digit means that digit repeats. Dots above the first and last digits of a group mean that whole group repeats. For example: \( 0.\dot{3} = 0.3333\ldots \) and \( 0.\dot{6}\dot{3} = 0.636363\ldots \) Strategy Perform long division. Divide the numerator by the denominator, continuing past the decimal point. Watch the remainders — when a remainder repeats, you have found the recurring block. For a mixed number, convert the fractional part only, then attach the whole number. (a) \( \dfrac{1}{6} \) Step 1 — Divide 1 by 6 step by step \( 1 \div 6 \): since \( 1 < 6 \), place a decimal point and work with 10, then 40, and so on. • \( 10 \div 6 = 1 \) remainder \( 4 \) → write digit 1 after the decimal point • Append a zero to the remainder: \( 40 \div 6 = 6 \) remainder \( 4 \) → write digit 6 • Append a zero to the remainder: \( 40 \div 6 = 6 \) remainder \( 4 \) → write digit 6 again The remainder 4 never changes, so the digit 6 repeats forever. \[ \frac{1}{6} = 0.1\dot{6} = 0.1666\ldots \] (b) \( \dfrac{7}{11} \) Step 2 — Divide 7 by 11 step by step \( 7 \div 11 \): since \( 7 < 11 \), place a decimal point and work with 70, then 40, then 70 again. • \( 70 \div 11 = 6 \) remainder \( 4 \) → write digit 6 • Append a zero to the remainder: \( 40 \div 11 = 3 \) remainder \( 7 \) → write digit 3 • Append a zero to the remainder: \( 70 \div 11 = 6 \) remainder \( 4 \) → back to the start The remainder cycle \( 4 \to 7 \to 4 \to \cdots \) repeats, so the two-digit block "63" recurs forever. \[ \frac{7}{11} = 0.\dot{6}\dot{3} = 0.6363\ldots \] (c) \( 3\dfrac{8}{15} \) Step 3 — Separate the whole number and convert the fraction Write \( 3\dfrac{8}{15} = 3 + \dfrac{8}{15} \). Now divide 8 by 15 step by step: • \( 80 \div 15 = 5 \) remainder \( 5 \) → write digit 5 • Append a zero to the remainder: \( 50 \div 15 = 3 \) remainder \( 5 \) → write digit 3 • Append a zero to the remainder: \( 50 \div 15 = 3 \) remainder \( 5 \) → back to the same remainder The remainder 5 repeats indefinitely, so only the digit 3 recurs. \[ \frac{8}{15} = 0.5\dot{3} \] Step 4 — Attach the whole number \[ 3\frac{8}{15} = 3 + 0.5\dot{3} = 3.5\dot{3} \] Final Answer (a) \( \dfrac{1}{6} = 0.1\dot{6} \) (b) \( \dfrac{7}{11} = 0.\dot{6}\dot{3} \) (c) \( 3\dfrac{8}{15} = 3.5\dot{3} \) ◀ Previous Question 3 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 4 of 13 Next ▶ Question 4 🟢 Easy

Write down the value and the approximate value correct to 4 decimal places:

  1. \( 12 \)
  2. \( 0.\dot{2}\dot{5} \)
  3. \( 1.\dot{3}\dot{4} \)
  4. \( 5.1\dot{3}0\dot{2} \)
Theory — Approximating to Decimal Places To approximate a number to 4 decimal places: 1. Write out the decimal until you have at least 5 digits after the point. 2. Look at the 5th decimal digit:    • If it is 5 or more → round the 4th digit up by 1.    • If it is less than 5 → leave the 4th digit unchanged (round down). 3. Drop all digits from the 5th place onward. Strategy First write the full decimal expansion by identifying the repeating block. Then read the first four decimal places, check the fifth, and round accordingly. (a) \( 12 \) Step 1 — Write the exact value and approximate 12 is a whole number. Its decimal expansion is \( 12.000000\ldots \) — all zeros, no recurring part. Exact value: \( 12 \) Approximate value (4 d.p.): \( 12.0000 \) (b) \( 0.\dot{2}\dot{5} \) Step 2 — Write the expansion and approximate \( 0.\dot{2}\dot{5} \) means the block "25" repeats: \[ 0.\dot{2}\dot{5} = 0.25252525\ldots \] The decimal digits are: 2, 5, 2, 5, 2, 5, … 4 decimal places: 0.2525 5th digit: 2 → less than 5, so round down (keep 4th digit as 5). Approximate value (4 d.p.): \( 0.2525 \) (c) \( 1.\dot{3}\dot{4} \) Step 3 — Write the expansion and approximate \( 1.\dot{3}\dot{4} \) means the block "34" repeats: \[ 1.\dot{3}\dot{4} = 1.34343434\ldots \] Decimal digits after the point: 3, 4, 3, 4, 3, 4, … 4 decimal places: 1.3434 5th digit: 3 → less than 5, so round down. Approximate value (4 d.p.): \( 1.3434 \) (d) \( 5.1\dot{3}0\dot{2} \) Step 4 — Write the expansion and approximate \( 5.1\dot{3}0\dot{2} \) means the block "302" repeats after the initial 1: \[ 5.1\dot{3}0\dot{2} = 5.1302302302\ldots \] Decimal digits after the point: 1, 3, 0, 2, 3, 0, 2, … 4 decimal places: 5.1302 5th digit: 3 → less than 5, so round down. Approximate value (4 d.p.): \( 5.1302 \) Final Answer (a) \( 12 \) — exact: \( 12 \), approx: \( 12.0000 \) (b) \( 0.\dot{2}\dot{5} = 0.2525\overline{25}\ldots \) — approx: \( 0.2525 \) (c) \( 1.\dot{3}\dot{4} = 1.3434\overline{34}\ldots \) — approx: \( 1.3434 \) (d) \( 5.1\dot{3}0\dot{2} = 5.1302\overline{302}\ldots \) — approx: \( 5.1302 \) ◀ Previous Question 4 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 5 of 13 Next ▶ Question 5 🟡 Medium

Convert the following recurring decimals into simple fractions:

  1. \( 0.\dot{2} \)
  2. \( 0.3\dot{5} \)
  3. \( 3.7\dot{8} \)
Theory — The Algebraic Method The trick is to use multiplication to shift the decimal so that two versions of the same number can be subtracted, making the recurring part disappear. Key rule: If there are \( d \) non-recurring decimal digits and \( n \) recurring digits, multiply by \( 10^{d+n} \) and by \( 10^d \), then subtract. This cancels the recurring tail. Formula — Recurring Decimal to Fraction For a recurring decimal with \( d \) non-recurring digits after the point and \( n \) recurring digits: \[ \text{Fraction} = \frac{\text{(all digits up to end of one period)} - \text{(non-recurring digits only)}}{\underbrace{99\ldots9}_{n}\underbrace{00\ldots0}_{d}} \] The denominator has \( n \) nines followed by \( d \) zeros. Strategy Let \( x \) equal the recurring decimal. Multiply by the right powers of 10 to shift the decimal, then subtract to eliminate the repeating part. Solve for \( x \) and simplify the fraction. (a) \( 0.\dot{2} \) Step 1 — Set up the equation Let \( x = 0.\dot{2} = 0.2222\ldots \) There are 0 non-recurring digits and 1 recurring digit, so multiply by \( 10^1 = 10 \): \[ 10x = 2.2222\ldots \] Step 2 — Subtract to remove the recurring part \[ 10x - x = 2.2222\ldots - 0.2222\ldots \] \[ 9x = 2 \] \[ x = \frac{2}{9} \] (b) \( 0.3\dot{5} \) Step 3 — Set up the equation Let \( x = 0.3\dot{5} = 0.35555\ldots \) There is 1 non-recurring digit (3) and 1 recurring digit (5). Multiply by \( 10^1 = 10 \) and by \( 10^{1+1} = 100 \): \[ 10x = 3.5555\ldots \] \[ 100x = 35.5555\ldots \] Step 4 — Subtract to remove the recurring part \[ 100x - 10x = 35.5555\ldots - 3.5555\ldots \] \[ 90x = 32 \] \[ x = \frac{32}{90} = \frac{16}{45} \] (c) \( 3.7\dot{8} \) Step 5 — Set up the equation Let \( x = 3.7\dot{8} = 3.7888\ldots \) There is 1 non-recurring digit after the decimal point (7) and 1 recurring digit (8). Multiply by \( 10 \) and by \( 100 \): \[ 10x = 37.888\ldots \] \[ 100x = 378.888\ldots \] Step 6 — Subtract and solve \[ 100x - 10x = 378.888\ldots - 37.888\ldots \] \[ 90x = 341 \] \[ x = \frac{341}{90} \] This fraction is already in its simplest form (GCD of 341 and 90 is 1). Note To check: \( \dfrac{341}{90} = 3.7888\ldots \) ✓ (divide 341 ÷ 90 to verify). Final Answer (a) \( 0.\dot{2} = \dfrac{2}{9} \) (b) \( 0.3\dot{5} = \dfrac{16}{45} \) (c) \( 3.7\dot{8} = \dfrac{341}{90} \) ◀ Previous Question 5 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 6 of 13 Next ▶ Question 6 🟡 Medium

Express the following as similar recurring decimal fractions:

\( 2.\dot{2}\dot{3} \) and \( 5.\dot{2}3\dot{5} \)

Theory — Similar Recurring Decimals Two recurring decimals are called similar when their repeating blocks have the same number of digits (the same period length). This matters for addition and subtraction: you can only add or subtract recurring decimals digit-by-digit when their periods are the same length. How to make them similar: If one decimal has period length \( p \) and another has period length \( q \), extend both periods to the LCM of \( p \) and \( q \) by writing the repeating block multiple times. Strategy 1. Find the period length of each decimal. 2. Find the LCM of those two period lengths. 3. Rewrite each decimal with the period repeated enough times so that the total period length equals the LCM. Step 1 — Identify the period of each decimal \( 2.\dot{2}\dot{3} = 2.232323\ldots \) → repeating block is "23" → period length = 2 \( 5.\dot{2}3\dot{5} = 5.235235235\ldots \) → repeating block is "235" → period length = 3 Step 2 — Find the LCM of the two period lengths LCM(2, 3) = 6 We need to rewrite both decimals so that their repeating block is 6 digits long. Step 3 — Extend each decimal to period length 6 For \( 2.\dot{2}\dot{3} \) (period 2, need period 6 = 3 repetitions): Write the block "23" three times: 23|23|23 \[ 2.\dot{2}\dot{3} = 2.\dot{2}3232\dot{3} \] For \( 5.\dot{2}3\dot{5} \) (period 3, need period 6 = 2 repetitions): Write the block "235" twice: 235|235 \[ 5.\dot{2}3\dot{5} = 5.\dot{2}3523\dot{5} \] Both now have period length 6, so they are similar. Note Repeating the block does not change the value of the decimal — it just rewrites it with a longer period. For example, \( 0.\dot{3} = 0.\dot{3}\dot{3} = 0.333\ldots \) regardless of how you group it. Final Answer \( 2.\dot{2}\dot{3} = 2.\dot{2}3232\dot{3} \) \( 5.\dot{2}3\dot{5} = 5.\dot{2}3523\dot{5} \) Both now have period length 6 and are similar recurring decimals. ◀ Previous Question 6 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 7 of 13 Next ▶ Question 7 🟡 Medium

Add: \( 0.4\dot{5} + 0.1\dot{3}\dot{4} \)

Strategy Convert each recurring decimal to a fraction using the algebraic method, add the fractions, then convert the result back to a decimal. This is more reliable than trying to add recurring decimals directly. Step 1 — Convert \( 0.4\dot{5} \) to a fraction Let \( x = 0.4\dot{5} = 0.45555\ldots \) 1 non-recurring digit (4), 1 recurring digit (5) → multiply by 10 and 100: \[ 10x = 4.5555\ldots \] \[ 100x = 45.5555\ldots \] Subtract: \[ 90x = 41 \implies x = \frac{41}{90} \] Step 2 — Convert \( 0.1\dot{3}\dot{4} \) to a fraction Let \( x = 0.1\dot{3}\dot{4} = 0.13434\ldots \) 1 non-recurring digit (1), 2 recurring digits (34) → multiply by 10 and 1000: \[ 10x = 1.3434\ldots \] \[ 1000x = 134.3434\ldots \] Subtract: \[ 990x = 133 \implies x = \frac{133}{990} \] Step 3 — Add the two fractions \[ \frac{41}{90} + \frac{133}{990} \] The LCM of 90 and 990 is 990. Convert the first fraction: \[ \frac{41}{90} = \frac{41 \times 11}{990} = \frac{451}{990} \] Now add: \[ \frac{451}{990} + \frac{133}{990} = \frac{584}{990} \] Simplify (GCD of 584 and 990 is 2): \[ \frac{584}{990} = \frac{292}{495} \] Step 4 — Convert the result back to a decimal Divide 292 by 495 using long division: \( 2920 \div 495 = 5 \) remainder 445 \( 4450 \div 495 = 8 \) remainder 490 \( 4900 \div 495 = 9 \) remainder 445 \( 4450 \div 495 = 8 \) remainder 490 — the pair (8, 9) repeats. \[ \frac{292}{495} = 0.5\dot{8}\dot{9} \] Final Answer \[ 0.4\dot{5} + 0.1\dot{3}\dot{4} = \frac{292}{495} = 0.5\dot{8}\dot{9} \] ◀ Previous Question 7 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 8 of 13 Next ▶ Question 8 🟡 Medium

Subtract:

  1. \( 3.\dot{4} - 2.1\dot{3} \)
  2. \( 8.49 - 5.35\dot{6} \)
Strategy Convert each recurring decimal to a fraction, perform the subtraction as a fraction calculation, then convert the answer back to a decimal. This avoids any confusion with misaligned repeating blocks. (a) \( 3.\dot{4} - 2.1\dot{3} \) Step 1 — Convert \( 3.\dot{4} \) to a fraction Let \( x = 3.\dot{4} = 3.4444\ldots \) 0 non-recurring digits, 1 recurring digit → multiply by 10: \[ 10x = 34.\dot{4} \] \[ 10x - x = 34.\dot{4} - 3.\dot{4} \implies 9x = 31 \implies x = \frac{31}{9} \] Step 2 — Convert \( 2.1\dot{3} \) to a fraction Let \( x = 2.1\dot{3} = 2.1333\ldots \) 1 non-recurring digit (1), 1 recurring digit (3) → multiply by 10 and 100: \[ 10x = 21.333\ldots \qquad 100x = 213.333\ldots \] \[ 90x = 192 \implies x = \frac{192}{90} = \frac{32}{15} \] Step 3 — Subtract the fractions \[ \frac{31}{9} - \frac{32}{15} \] LCM(9, 15) = 45: \[ \frac{31 \times 5}{45} - \frac{32 \times 3}{45} = \frac{155 - 96}{45} = \frac{59}{45} \] Converting: \( 59 \div 45 = 1 \) rem 14; \( 140 \div 45 = 3 \) rem 5; \( 50 \div 45 = 1 \) rem 5 (repeats). \[ \frac{59}{45} = 1.3\dot{1} \] (b) \( 8.49 - 5.35\dot{6} \) Step 4 — Convert \( 5.35\dot{6} \) to a fraction Let \( x = 5.35\dot{6} = 5.35666\ldots \) 2 non-recurring digits (35), 1 recurring digit (6) → multiply by 100 and 1000: \[ 100x = 535.666\ldots \qquad 1000x = 5356.666\ldots \] \[ 900x = 4821 \implies x = \frac{4821}{900} = \frac{1607}{300} \] Step 5 — Subtract the fractions Write \( 8.49 = \dfrac{849}{100} \). The LCM of 100 and 300 is 300: \[ \frac{849}{100} - \frac{1607}{300} = \frac{2547}{300} - \frac{1607}{300} = \frac{940}{300} = \frac{47}{15} \] Converting: \( 47 \div 15 = 3 \) rem 2; \( 20 \div 15 = 1 \) rem 5; \( 50 \div 15 = 3 \) rem 5 (repeats). \[ \frac{47}{15} = 3.1\dot{3} \] Final Answer (a) \( 3.\dot{4} - 2.1\dot{3} = \dfrac{59}{45} = 1.3\dot{1} \) (b) \( 8.49 - 5.35\dot{6} = \dfrac{47}{15} = 3.1\dot{3} \) ◀ Previous Question 8 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 9 of 13 Next ▶ Question 9 🔴 Hard

Multiply:

  1. \( 0.\dot{3} \times 0.\dot{6} \)
  2. \( 2.4 \times 0.\dot{8}\dot{1} \)
Strategy Convert every recurring decimal to a fraction. Multiply the fractions using \( \dfrac{a}{b} \times \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{ac}{bd} \). Simplify the result and convert back to a decimal. (a) \( 0.\dot{3} \times 0.\dot{6} \) Step 1 — Convert both decimals to fractions \[ 0.\dot{3} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3} \qquad 0.\dot{6} = \frac{6}{9} = \frac{2}{3} \] Step 2 — Multiply the fractions \[ \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{2}{3} = \frac{2}{9} = 0.\dot{2} \] (b) \( 2.4 \times 0.\dot{8}\dot{1} \) Step 3 — Convert both numbers to fractions \[ 2.4 = \frac{24}{10} = \frac{12}{5} \] For \( 0.\dot{8}\dot{1} = 0.818181\ldots \), the repeating block "81" has 2 digits, so multiply by 99: \[ 99x = 81 \implies x = \frac{81}{99} = \frac{9}{11} \] Step 4 — Multiply the fractions \[ \frac{12}{5} \times \frac{9}{11} = \frac{108}{55} \] Step 5 — Convert \( \dfrac{108}{55} \) back to a decimal \( 108 \div 55 = 1 \) rem 53 \( 530 \div 55 = 9 \) rem 35 \( 350 \div 55 = 6 \) rem 20 \( 200 \div 55 = 3 \) rem 35 → remainder 35 repeats, so block "63" recurs. \[ \frac{108}{55} = 1.9\dot{6}\dot{3} \] Final Answer (a) \( 0.\dot{3} \times 0.\dot{6} = \dfrac{2}{9} = 0.\dot{2} \) (b) \( 2.4 \times 0.\dot{8}\dot{1} = \dfrac{108}{55} = 1.9\dot{6}\dot{3} \) ◀ Previous Question 9 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 10 of 13 Next ▶ Question 10 🔴 Hard

Divide:

  1. \( 0.\dot{3} \div 0.\dot{6} \)
  2. \( 2.\dot{3}\dot{7} \div 0.\dot{4}\dot{5} \)
Strategy Convert every recurring decimal to a fraction. Use the rule \( \dfrac{a}{b} \div \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{a}{b} \times \dfrac{d}{c} \) (multiply by the reciprocal). Simplify fully, then convert back to a decimal. (a) \( 0.\dot{3} \div 0.\dot{6} \) Step 1 — Convert to fractions and divide \[ 0.\dot{3} = \frac{1}{3}, \qquad 0.\dot{6} = \frac{2}{3} \] \[ \frac{1}{3} \div \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \] (b) \( 2.\dot{3}\dot{7} \div 0.\dot{4}\dot{5} \) Step 2 — Convert \( 2.\dot{3}\dot{7} \) to a fraction Let \( x = 2.\dot{3}\dot{7} = 2.373737\ldots \) 0 non-recurring digits, 2 recurring digits → multiply by 100: \[ 100x = 237.\dot{3}\dot{7} \] \[ 100x - x = 235 \implies 99x = 235 \implies x = \frac{235}{99} \] Step 3 — Convert \( 0.\dot{4}\dot{5} \) to a fraction Let \( x = 0.\dot{4}\dot{5} = 0.454545\ldots \) \[ 99x = 45 \implies x = \frac{45}{99} = \frac{5}{11} \] Step 4 — Divide the fractions \[ \frac{235}{99} \div \frac{5}{11} = \frac{235}{99} \times \frac{11}{5} = \frac{235 \times 11}{99 \times 5} = \frac{2585}{495} \] Simplify. The GCD of 2585 and 495: \[ 2585 = 5 \times 11 \times 47, \qquad 495 = 5 \times 9 \times 11 \] GCD = \( 5 \times 11 = 55 \): \[ \frac{2585}{495} = \frac{47}{9} \] Step 5 — Convert \( \dfrac{47}{9} \) to a decimal \( 47 \div 9 = 5 \) rem 2; \( 20 \div 9 = 2 \) rem 2 (repeats immediately). \[ \frac{47}{9} = 5.\dot{2} \] Final Answer (a) \( 0.\dot{3} \div 0.\dot{6} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \) (b) \( 2.\dot{3}\dot{7} \div 0.\dot{4}\dot{5} = \dfrac{47}{9} = 5.\dot{2} \) ◀ Previous Question 10 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 11 of 13 Next ▶ Question 11 🔴 Hard

Prove that the following numbers are irrational:

  1. \( \sqrt{5} \)
  2. \( \sqrt{10} \)
Theory — Proof by Contradiction A proof by contradiction works like this: 1. Assume the opposite of what you want to prove is true. 2. Follow the logical consequences of that assumption. 3. Arrive at something that is impossible or contradicts your assumption. 4. Conclude that your assumption must have been false — so the original statement is true. We also use the fact: if a prime \( p \) divides \( n^2 \), then \( p \) divides \( n \). This follows because if \( n = p_1^{a_1} p_2^{a_2}\cdots \), then \( n^2 = p_1^{2a_1}p_2^{2a_2}\cdots \) — a prime that appears in \( n^2 \) must have already appeared in \( n \). Strategy Assume \( \sqrt{5} \) (and then \( \sqrt{10} \)) is rational, i.e. it equals \( \dfrac{p}{q} \) in lowest terms (where \( p \) and \( q \) share no common factor). Square both sides, then show that both \( p \) and \( q \) must share a common factor — contradicting the assumption. (a) Prove \( \sqrt{5} \) is irrational Step 1 — Assume the opposite Suppose, for contradiction, that \( \sqrt{5} \) is rational. Then we can write \( \sqrt{5} = \dfrac{p}{q} \) where \( p \) and \( q \) are integers with no common factor (the fraction is in its lowest terms) and \( q \neq 0 \). Step 2 — Square both sides \[ \left(\sqrt{5}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{p}{q}\right)^2 \implies 5 = \frac{p^2}{q^2} \implies p^2 = 5q^2 \] This tells us that 5 divides \( p^2 \). Step 3 — Show 5 divides p Since 5 is prime and \( 5 \mid p^2 \), we conclude \( 5 \mid p \). So write \( p = 5k \) for some integer \( k \). Step 4 — Substitute and find the contradiction Substitute \( p = 5k \) into \( p^2 = 5q^2 \): \[ (5k)^2 = 5q^2 \implies 25k^2 = 5q^2 \implies q^2 = 5k^2 \] So 5 divides \( q^2 \), and since 5 is prime, \( 5 \mid q \). But now both \( p \) and \( q \) are divisible by 5. This contradicts our assumption that \( \dfrac{p}{q} \) is in its lowest terms. Therefore \( \sqrt{5} \) is irrational. \( \blacksquare \) (b) Prove \( \sqrt{10} \) is irrational Step 5 — Assume the opposite Suppose \( \sqrt{10} = \dfrac{p}{q} \) in lowest terms (\( p, q \) share no common factor, \( q \neq 0 \)). Step 6 — Square both sides \[ 10 = \frac{p^2}{q^2} \implies p^2 = 10q^2 \] Since \( 10q^2 \) is even, \( p^2 \) is even, which means \( p \) is even (an odd number squared is always odd). Write \( p = 2k \). Step 7 — Find the contradiction Substitute \( p = 2k \): \[ (2k)^2 = 10q^2 \implies 4k^2 = 10q^2 \implies 2k^2 = 5q^2 \] The left side is even. For \( 5q^2 \) to be even, since 5 is odd, \( q^2 \) must be even, so \( q \) is even. Both \( p \) and \( q \) are divisible by 2, contradicting lowest terms. Therefore \( \sqrt{10} \) is irrational. \( \blacksquare \) Final Answer Both \( \sqrt{5} \) and \( \sqrt{10} \) are irrational. In each case, assuming rationality leads to a contradiction: both \( p \) and \( q \) end up sharing a common factor, which is impossible if the fraction is in lowest terms. ◀ Previous Question 11 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 12 of 13 Question 12 🔴 Hard

Prove that the product of any two consecutive even integers is divisible by 8.

Theory — Consecutive Even Integers An even integer can always be written as \( 2n \) for some integer \( n \). The next even integer after \( 2n \) is \( 2n + 2 = 2(n+1) \). Key fact: among any two consecutive integers \( n \) and \( n+1 \), exactly one is even and one is odd. Therefore their product \( n(n+1) \) is always even — it equals \( 2m \) for some integer \( m \). Strategy Write the two consecutive even integers in general form as \( 2n \) and \( 2(n+1) \). Multiply them, then factor the result to show it is a multiple of 8. Step 1 — Write the two consecutive even integers in general form Let the two consecutive even integers be \( 2n \) and \( 2(n+1) \), where \( n \) is any integer. Step 2 — Write out their product \[ \text{Product} = 2n \times 2(n+1) = 4n(n+1) \] Step 3 — Show that \( n(n+1) \) is always even \( n \) and \( n+1 \) are consecutive integers. One of them must be even (integers alternate between odd and even). The product of any even number with anything is even, so: \[ n(n+1) = 2m \quad \text{for some integer } m \] Step 4 — Conclude the product is divisible by 8 Substitute \( n(n+1) = 2m \) into the product: \[ \text{Product} = 4 \times n(n+1) = 4 \times 2m = 8m \] Since the product equals \( 8m \) for some integer \( m \), it is divisible by 8. \( \blacksquare \) Note — Quick Check Try it with examples: \( 2 \times 4 = 8 \) ✓, \( 4 \times 6 = 24 = 8 \times 3 \) ✓, \( 6 \times 8 = 48 = 8 \times 6 \) ✓. The general proof covers all cases at once. Final Answer Any two consecutive even integers can be written as \( 2n \) and \( 2(n+1) \). Their product is: \[ 2n \cdot 2(n+1) = 4n(n+1) \] Since \( n \) and \( n+1 \) are consecutive, one is even, so \( n(n+1) = 2m \) for some integer \( m \). Therefore the product equals \( 8m \), which is divisible by 8. \( \blacksquare \) ◀ Previous Question 12 of 13 Next ▶ ◀ Previous Question 13 of 13 Question 13 🟡 Medium
  1. Find two irrational numbers between \( 0.12 \) and \( 0.31 \).
  2. Find a rational number and an irrational number between \( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \) and \( \sqrt{2} \).
Theory — Density of Rational and Irrational Numbers Between any two distinct real numbers, no matter how close, there are infinitely many rational numbers and infinitely many irrational numbers. This property is called density. To find an irrational in a range, a practical method is to take a known irrational (like \( \sqrt{2} \) or \( \sqrt{3} \)) and scale it by a rational number to land inside the target range. Strategy For part (a): divide a known irrational (\( \sqrt{2} \) or \( \sqrt{3} \)) by a suitable integer so the result falls between 0.12 and 0.31. A rational times an irrational remains irrational. For part (b): estimate \( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \) and \( \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \). Pick one obvious rational and one irrational in that interval, then verify algebraically. (a) Two irrational numbers between 0.12 and 0.31 Step 1 — Scale known irrationals into the target range \( \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41421\ldots \) and \( \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73205\ldots \) Dividing each by 10: \[ \frac{\sqrt{2}}{10} \approx 0.14142\ldots \qquad \frac{\sqrt{3}}{10} \approx 0.17320\ldots \] Both lie strictly between 0.12 and 0.31. ✓ Step 2 — Confirm both are irrational \( \sqrt{2} \) is irrational (its decimal expansion never terminates or repeats). Dividing by the non-zero rational 10 keeps it irrational — a rational divided into an irrational never produces a rational result. The same reasoning applies to \( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{10} \). (b) A rational and an irrational between \( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \) and \( \sqrt{2} \) Step 3 — Estimate the endpoints \[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx \frac{1.41421}{2} \approx 0.7071 \] \[ \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142 \] We need one rational and one irrational strictly between \( 0.7071 \) and \( 1.4142 \). Step 4 — Choose a rational number in the range The whole number 1 satisfies \( 0.707 < 1 < 1.414 \). Rational number: \( 1 \) Step 5 — Choose and verify an irrational number in the range Try \( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx \dfrac{1.73205}{2} \approx 0.8660 \). Left inequality — show \( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} < \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \): \[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} < \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \iff \sqrt{2} < \sqrt{3} \iff 2 < 3 \;\checkmark \] Right inequality — show \( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} < \sqrt{2} \): \[ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} < \sqrt{2} \iff \sqrt{3} < 2\sqrt{2} \iff 3 < 8 \;\checkmark \] Since \( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \) is irrational ÷ rational, it is irrational. Irrational number: \( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \) Final Answer (a) Two irrationals between 0.12 and 0.31: \(\;\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{10} \approx 0.1414\ldots\) and \(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{10} \approx 0.1732\ldots\) (b) Between \( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \) and \( \sqrt{2} \):    Rational: \( 1 \)    Irrational: \( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660\ldots \) ◀ Previous Question 13 of 13
